title: The King of Tyre and the Promise of Restoration

sections.header: Verses 1-10 — The Prince of Tyre: Human Pride

heb.paragraph: The prince of Tyre declares 'I am a god' (el ani, v.2). God’s response is devastating: 'You are a man, not a god' — the fundamental distinction that human pride seeks to erase.

panels.ctx: The oracle shifts from Tyre as a city to Tyre’s ruler personally. The 'prince' (nagid) claims divine status because of his wisdom in amassing wealth. God establishes the fundamental Creator-creature distinction.

cross.note: The serpent’s original temptation: 'you will be like God.' The prince of Tyre repeats the primal sin.
The king of Babylon declares 'I will make myself like the Most High.'
Herod Agrippa accepts divine acclamation and is struck down.

notes.note: The prince claims to be 'a god, sitting on the throne of a god.' Ultimate hubris.
The reference to Daniel may refer to the legendary wise man of Ugaritic texts or the biblical Daniel.
The 'most ruthless of nations' will strip the prince of his beauty and cast him into the pit.
The devastating question: 'Will you then say I am a god in the presence of those who kill you?'
Calvin sees the prince as the archetype of human pride: wealth leads to self-sufficiency, then self-deification.
Calvin: Death is the great equaliser that strips all pretensions of divinity.
The Hebrew nagid ('prince') rather than melek ('king') may be a deliberate demotion.
The Hebrew dn’l may refer to the legendary Dan’el of Ugaritic literature.
Block identifies this as a 'hubris oracle' with deep ancient Near Eastern parallels.
Zimmerli reads this as a 'wisdom-critical' text: the prince’s wisdom is real but misused.

sections.header: Verses 11-19 — The King of Tyre: Eden and the Fall

heb.paragraph: The king was 'an anointed guardian cherub' on 'the holy mountain of God.' The cherub language connects to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:24) and the tabernacle theology (Exod 25:18-22).
The king is placed 'in Eden, the garden of God.' The Eden imagery elevates the oracle beyond historical commentary into theological territory.

panels.ctx: This is one of the most debated passages in Ezekiel. The 'king' (melek, now elevated from 'prince') is described in Edenic and angelic language. Many interpreters have read this as a description of Satan’s fall; others read it as mythological language applied to a human ruler.

cross.note: The Eden imagery directly connects to Genesis 3: original perfection, guardian cherub, fall through pride, and expulsion.
The 'morning star' passage parallels Ezekiel 28: original glory, fall through pride. Both are traditionally read as reflecting Satan’s fall.
Jesus says 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.'
The war in heaven and Satan’s expulsion echoes the expulsion from the mountain of God.

notes.note: The lament uses superlative language: 'You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty.'
The nine precious stones echo the high priest’s breastplate (Exod 28:17-20) in different order.
The 'anointed cherub who covers' uses tabernacle language. Many see a reference to Satan’s original exalted position.
Blameless until wickedness was found. The fall pattern: beauty to vanity, wisdom to pride, trade to violence.
Calvin is cautious about the Satan interpretation, reading this primarily as hyperbolic language while acknowledging the language transcends any merely human referent.
Calvin: Beauty, when it breeds self-admiration, becomes the seed of its own destruction.
The MT lists nine stones; the LXX lists twelve, matching the high priest’s breastplate. The LXX may preserve an earlier reading.
The phrase 'anointed guardian cherub' is extremely difficult. The word mimshakh may mean 'anointed' or 'outspread.'
Block reads this as a 'cosmic allegory' in which the king re-enacts the Adamic fall.
Zimmerli argues this draws on a pre-Israelite myth of a primordial man in paradise — not identical to Genesis 2-3 but from the same tradition pool.

sections.header: Verses 20-26 — Oracle Against Sidon and Israel’s Restoration

heb.paragraph: God declares 'I will be proved holy' (niqdashti) through Sidon’s judgment. The niphal form indicates that God’s holiness is demonstrated through his acts of justice.

panels.ctx: The brief Sidon oracle functions as a transition from the Tyre oracles to the restoration promise. The chapter concludes with the first explicit restoration oracle since the judgment sections began.

cross.note: The restoration promise anticipates the fuller vision of Ezekiel 36.
Jeremiah’s parallel promise: Jacob will return, live in peace.
Zechariah’s vision of Jerusalem without walls — God himself will be a wall of fire.

notes.note: God will 'gain glory' through Sidon’s judgment.
No more 'painful briers or sharp thorns' from neighbouring nations.
The restoration promise: gathering from nations, dwelling in safety, building houses, planting vineyards.
Calvin reads the restoration as ultimately fulfilled in Christ and the church.
The phrase 'gathered from the nations' uses ingathering language that becomes technical for eschatological restoration.
Block identifies the restoration oracle as structurally crucial: the counterbalance to chapters 25-28’s relentless judgment.
Zimmerli argues the restoration oracle may be a later editorial addition that provides the necessary theological counterweight.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as God brings down the proudest human claims to divinity. The mercy score rises with the restoration promise.

rows.label: vv.1-10
rows.text: The Prince of Tyre: Human Hubris

rows.label: vv.11-19
rows.text: The King of Tyre: Edenic Fall

lit.note: The chapter moves from human pride to cosmic fall to the first restoration promise, pivoting the book from judgment toward hope.

hebtext.note: The prince of Tyre declares 'I am a god' (el ani, v.2). God’s response is devastating: 'You are a man, not a god' — the fundamental distinction that human pride seeks to erase.
The king was 'an anointed guardian cherub' on 'the holy mountain of God.' The cherub language connects to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:24) and the tabernacle theology (Exod 25:18-22).
The king is placed 'in Eden, the garden of God.' The Eden imagery elevates the oracle beyond historical commentary into theological territory.
God declares 'I will be proved holy' (niqdashti) through Sidon’s judgment. The niphal form indicates that God’s holiness is demonstrated through his acts of justice.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The King of Tyre and the Promise of Restoration

translations.text: “ ‘Therefore this is what the Sovereign Lord says: “ ‘Because you think you are wise, as wise as a god,
therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Because you make your heart like the heart of a god,
I am going to bring foreigners against you, the most ruthless of nations; they will draw their swords against your beauty and wisdom and pierce your shining splendor.
therefore, behold, I will bring foreigners upon you, the most ruthless of the nations; and they shall draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom and defile your splendor.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The prince claims to be 'a god, sitting on the throne of a god.' Ultimate hubris."
